prompt: International travel has many advantages to both travellers and the country visited. Do you think the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?
intro_note: One-sided rewrite of the original W4 essay. Both bodies argue for advantages; drawbacks are conceded briefly then rebutted, so the "outweigh" position is held end-to-end. Hover any highlighted phrase for a band-7 craft note.

spans.text: Crossing borders for leisure or work

highlight.label: Topic paraphrase
highlight.explanation: Recasts "international travel" → "crossing borders for leisure or work" without copying the prompt. A fresh paraphrase opener is one of the cheapest Band-7 TR signals — markers see it in the first sentence.

spans.text: has become far more common than it once was, and it clearly affects both the people who travel and the nations that receive them.
Although international travel is not without its problems

highlight.label: Concession opener (litotes)
highlight.explanation: "not without its problems" = double negative for understated acknowledgement. Combined with "Although…", it concedes briefly so the upcoming thesis sounds weighed rather than naïve.

spans.text: I firmly believe that its benefits outweigh its drawbacks

highlight.label: Band-7 thesis register
highlight.explanation: Replaces the original's "I opine that" (register-odd) with a natural stance verb + intensifier. Anchors a clear position the conclusion can echo without verbatim restatement.

spans.text: , because it
broadens travellers' horizons

highlight.label: Natural collocation
highlight.explanation: Strong figurative collocation (broaden + horizons). Band 7 LR rewards "less-common items used accurately" — this one is idiomatic and unambiguous.

spans.text: and
brings valuable income to host countries

highlight.label: Body roadmap
highlight.explanation: Names the TWO reasons the bodies will develop — a mini-outline that prevents "listing" drift. Body 2 should match this phrase, not invent a new direction.

spans.text: The most obvious advantage for travellers themselves is

highlight.label: PEEL — Point
highlight.explanation: Body topic sentence states ONE main idea (advantage to travellers). Replaces the original's fronted sequencer "First and foremost" with content-driven framing.

spans.text: the chance to experience cultures that cannot be found at home.
By living among local people, even briefly

highlight.label: Participle-clause opener
highlight.explanation: "By + V-ing" fronts a non-finite clause — exactly the showcase structure §7 in `band7-grammar-showcases.md` wants. The original essay uses only finite "although/because/while" subordinators.

spans.text: , visitors encounter different languages, cuisines and customs first-hand,
which tends to make them more open-minded and adaptable

highlight.label: Non-defining relative + hedged claim
highlight.explanation: "which tends to…" attaches a non-defining relative clause AND softens the claim with "tends to" — markers credit both range and tentative phrasing.

spans.text: A student who spends a summer abroad, for instance

highlight.label: PEEL — Example
highlight.explanation: Concrete example (a student, a summer abroad) anchors the abstract claim. The original essay names ideas like "new experience" but never illustrates them — the gap that holds it at TR 6.

spans.text: , often returns
not only with improved language skills but also with a broader perspective

highlight.label: Correlative "not only…but also"
highlight.explanation: Paired conjunction balances two outcomes in one sentence — flexible cohesion within the sentence, not just between sentences. A Band-7 alternative to "Also," / "In addition,".

spans.text: on how others live.
Such personal growth

highlight.label: Content link (referencing)
highlight.explanation: "Such + noun phrase" links back to the prior sentence by summarising its content — exactly the §3 fix in `cohesion-upgrades.md` for the original's vague "this tactic".

spans.text: is difficult to gain from books or films alone, and it stays with travellers long after the trip ends.
International tourism is,
in addition

highlight.label: Mid-sentence connective
highlight.explanation: Embedded "in addition" (not fronted) replaces the original's "Secondly," / "Furthermore," pattern. Band 7 CC = flexible placement, not mechanical sequencers.

spans.text: a powerful source of revenue

highlight.label: Topic-bank collocation
highlight.explanation: From the tourism topic bank — "source of revenue", later "tourism receipts". Replaces the original's repeated "maximise the revenue".

spans.text: for the destinations involved. Money spent by visitors on accommodation, transport and food flows directly into local businesses,
creating jobs and encouraging governments to invest

highlight.label: Present-participle result clause
highlight.explanation: "-ing" clause expresses RESULT without starting a new sentence — packs cause-and-effect efficiently and adds GRA range beyond finite subordination.

spans.text: in roads, airports and public services. In many regions, tourism is in fact
the single largest employer

highlight.label: Precise collocation
highlight.explanation: "single largest employer" is a standard, journalistic collocation — concrete and verifiable, not vague ("helps the economy").

spans.text: , supporting families
who would otherwise struggle to find stable work

highlight.label: Counterfactual with "would"
highlight.explanation: "would + otherwise" sets up a hypothetical alternative — a small conditional-like structure that adds range without writing a full "If…" sentence.

spans.text: This economic boost

highlight.label: Content link (summary noun)
highlight.explanation: "This + summary noun phrase" (economic boost) refers cleanly back to the prior paragraph's main idea — replaces the mechanical "Furthermore," of the original.

spans.text: benefits not just the tourism sector but the wider community.
Admittedly

highlight.label: Concession marker
highlight.explanation: Signals "I will now ADMIT the other side" — the move that turns the essay from listing into weighing. Without it, an outweigh-question essay reads as 50/50.

spans.text: , international travel can create difficulties,
such as overtourism in popular cities

highlight.label: Specific concession
highlight.explanation: Names the strongest opposing point (overtourism) BEFORE rebutting it. Conceding only weak points looks evasive — concede something real.

spans.text: or the vulnerability of visitors who do not speak the local language.
These concerns are real, yet they can largely be managed

highlight.label: Rebuttal pattern
highlight.explanation: "Concerns are real, YET…" — acknowledges before pushing back. This is the structural fix the feedback flagged for Band 6 → 7 TR uplift.

spans.text: through sensible regulation and better traveller awareness,
whereas the cultural and economic gains are lasting

highlight.label: Weighing contrast
highlight.explanation: "whereas" sets up an explicit comparison: manageable problems vs. lasting gains. This is what an *outweigh* question demands — a verdict that is ARGUED, not just asserted.

spans.text: In conclusion,
while the disadvantages of international travel should not be ignored

highlight.label: Concessive subordinator
highlight.explanation: "while + S + V" front-loads the concession in the conclusion too — keeps the weighing tone consistent end-to-end. Avoids the Band 6 trap of repeating the intro verbatim.

spans.text: I am convinced that

highlight.label: Varied stance verb
highlight.explanation: Uses a different stance phrase from the intro ("I firmly believe"). Repeating the exact intro phrase triggers the Band 6 descriptor "conclusions may be repetitive".

spans.text: the advantages are
considerably greater

highlight.label: Adverb-intensified comparison
highlight.explanation: "considerably + comparative" is more precise than "much more" and avoids the original's flat "more beneficial". Small lexical uplift, high frequency in Band-7 model answers.

spans.text: , since
travellers grow as individuals and host nations prosper economically

highlight.label: Paired result (parallel structure)
highlight.explanation: Parallel SVO clauses (travellers grow / nations prosper) restate the TWO body claims in fresh words — answers the prompt's TWO sides without copy-pasting the intro.
